case_name: United India Insurance Company Limited vs V.Vijayakumar on 8 September, 2010
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT MADRAS
compensation_amount: Rs.19,59,895/- together with interest at 9% per annum

compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal heads maintained; Court reallocated 'mental agony' (Rs.75,000) into 'pain and suffering' and enhanced that head to Rs.1,00,000; total unchanged. Insurer directed to pay and recover from owner.

facts: On 07.07.2002 at about 7:45 P.M. the claimant V. Vijayakumar was walking near Annur Bus Stand when a Tourist Maxi Cab (Mahendra van) bearing Regn.No. TN 27 L 3638 driven by the 3rd respondent dashed against him. He sustained severe head injuries including diffuse axonal injury and remained in hospital and ICU for months, ultimately assessed with 95% permanent disability and effectively bed-ridden. Claimant, then an accountant earning Rs.4,500/- per month, filed a claim petition for Rs.25,00,000/-. The vehicle owner and driver were ex parte; the insurer admitted policy existence but contended the driver lacked the required badge/authorisation to drive a transport (tourist) vehicle.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued the accident was caused by negligent driving of the Maxi Cab and the insurer failed to prove a breach of policy conditions sufficient to escape statutory liability. The claimant relied on evidence (eye-witness, FIR, criminal conviction) and medical records to establish negligence and severe disability, and submitted the insurer bears the burden to prove any fundamental breach by the insured.
arguments_respondent: The appellant insurer contended the driver held only an LMV licence and did not possess the badge/authorisation required to drive a transport/tourist Maxi Cab, thereby breaching policy conditions and absolving the insurer of liability. The insurer relied on R.W.1 (Motor Vehicles Inspector) and documentary evidence (Exs.B.1, B.2) to show lack of authorisation and urged the Tribunal should have fixed liability on owner/driver alone.
ratio_decidendi: "Chapter XI of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 providing compulsory insurance of vehicles against third-party risks is a social welfare legislation to extend relief by compensation to victims of accidents caused by use of motor vehicles." "The breach of policy condition e.g. disqualification of the driver or invalid driving licence of the driver, as contained in sub-section (2)(a)(ii) of Section 149, has to be proved to have been committed by the insured for avoiding liability by the insurer." "Even where the insurer is able to prove breach on the part of the insured concerning the policy condition regarding holding of a valid licence by the driver or his qualification to drive during the relevant period, the insurer would not be allowed to avoid its liability towards the insured unless the said breach or breaches ... is/are so fundamental as are found to have contributed to the cause of the accident." "The insurer who is made statutorily liable to pay compensation to third parties on account of the certificate of insurance issued shall be entitled to recover any such sum from the insured if the insurer were not otherwise liable to pay such sum to the insured by virtue of the conditions of the contract of insurance indicated by the policy." Applying these principles to the facts, the Court found the driver lacked authorisation to drive a transport vehicle (breach of policy) but, in the interest of the victim and applying the pay-and-recover doctrine, directed the insurer to satisfy the award and thereafter recover the amount from the owner.
final_order: The Tribunal's quantum award of Rs.19,59,895/- is confirmed. The appeal is partly allowed to the extent that the Appellant-Insurance Company is directed to pay the compensation with interest at 9% p.a. to the claimant and thereafter recover the amount from respondents 3 and 4 (owner/driver); the insurer must deposit the balance due within eight weeks.
summary: This is a Madras High Court appeal by United India Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.19,59,895 to V. Vijayakumar for catastrophic head injuries from a Maxi Cab accident on 07.07.2002. The insurer argued the driver only held an LMV licence and lacked the badge/authorisation to drive a transport vehicle, breaching policy conditions. The Court held the insurer had proved breach but applied the 'pay and recover' principle under Section 149: confirmed the compensation amount and directed the insurer to pay with 9% interest and recover from the owner. The claimant thus retains the award while the insurer may seek reimbursement from the insured.
embedding_summary: United India Insurance Company Ltd. v. V. Vijayakumar, High Court of Madras, C.M.A. No.3044 of 2005, judgment dated 08.09.2010. Accident on 07.07.2002 at Annur Bus Stand: Tourist Maxi Cab (Mahendra van Regn.No. TN 27 L 3638) hit pedestrian claimant causing severe head injury, diffuse axonal injury and 95% permanent disability. Claimant sought Rs.25,00,000; Tribunal awarded Rs.19,59,895 (breakdown: permanent disability Rs.8,20,800; medical Rs.9,37,095; loss of income Rs.1,00,000; pain/suffering and other heads). Key legal issues: validity of driver's licence/authorisation (LMV vs transport/badge), breach of policy condition under Section 149(2) M.V. Act 1988, burden on insurer to prove fundamental breach, and application of 'pay and recover' doctrine under Section 149 and Swaran Singh (2004) 3 SCC 297. Court relied on Swaran Singh, New India Assurance v. Kamla, Oriental Insurance v. Angad Kol, Kusum Rai, Laxmi Narain Dhut, Bhuvan Singh and others, held insurer proved lack of authorisation but nevertheless must pay award and may recover from owner; confirmed final compensation Rs.19,59,895 with interest at 9% p.a.
legal_principles: Pay-and-recover doctrine: insurer statutorily liable to pay third-party award and may recover from insured (Section 149) rather than avoid payment
Burden on insurer to prove breach of policy condition (e.g., invalid licence/badge) to escape liability
Mere absence/fake/invalid licence is not automatic defence; breach must be 'fundamental' and shown to have contributed to the accident
Tribunal empowered to adjudicate inter se disputes between insurer and insured and to issue recovery certificate (Section 168 / Section 174 remedies)
Distinct legal requirement that LMV licence does not authorise driving a transport vehicle; specific badge/authorisation is required under Tamil Nadu Rules
sections_cited: Section 2(21) and Section 2(47) Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (definitions)
Tamil Nadu Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989: Rule 7, Rule 10, Rule 12, Rule 21, Rule 35, Rule 36, Rule 37, Rule 40
cases_cited: National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh, (2004) 3 SCC 297
New India Assurance Company v. Kamla, 2001 (4) SCC 342 (AIR 2001 SC 1419)
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. vs. Angad Kol and Others, (2009 ACJ 1411)
National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Kusum Rai, 2006 ACJ 1336 (SC)
National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Laxmi Narain Dhut, 2007 A.C.J. 721 (SC)
Bhuvan Singh v. M/s. Oriental Insurance Company Ltd., J.T. 2009 (3) S.C. 333
United India Insurance Company Ltd. v. Veluchamy, (2005 (1) CTC 38)
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Prabhu Lal, (2008) 1 SCC 696
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Nanjappan, AIR 2004 SC 1630 = (2004) 13 SCC 224
bm25_text: United India Insurance Company Limited vs V.Vijayakumar on 8 September, 2010 IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT MADRAS 2010 On 07.07.2002 at about 7:45 P.M. the claimant V. Vijayakumar was walking near Annur Bus Stand when a Tourist Maxi Cab (Mahendra van) bearing Regn.No. TN 27 L 3638 driven by the 3rd respondent dashed against him. He sustained severe head injuries including diffuse axonal injury and remained in hospital and ICU for months, ultimately assessed with 95% permanent disability and effectively bed-ridden. Claimant, then an accountant earning Rs.4,500/- per month, filed a claim petition for Rs.25,00,000/-. The vehicle owner and driver were ex parte; the insurer admitted policy existence but contended the driver lacked the required badge/authorisation to drive a transport (tourist) vehicle. The claimant argued the accident was caused by negligent driving of the Maxi Cab and the insurer failed to prove a breach of policy conditions sufficient to escape statutory liability. The claimant relied on evidence (eye-witness, FIR, criminal conviction) and medical records to establish negligence and severe disability, and submitted the insurer bears the burden to prove any fundamental breach by the insured. The appellant insurer contended the driver held only an LMV licence and did not possess the badge/authorisation required to drive a transport/tourist Maxi Cab, thereby breaching policy conditions and absolving the insurer of liability. The insurer relied on R.W.1 (Motor Vehicles Inspector) and documentary evidence (Exs.B.1, B.2) to show lack of authorisation and urged the Tribunal should have fixed liability on owner/driver alone. "Chapter XI of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 providing compulsory insurance of vehicles against third-party risks is a social welfare legislation to extend relief by compensation to victims of accidents caused by use of motor vehicles." "The breach of policy condition e.g. disqualification of the driver or invalid driving licence of the driver, as contained in sub-section (2)(a)(ii) of Section 149, has to be proved to have been committed by the insured for avoiding liability by the insurer." "Even where the insurer is able to prove breach on the part of the insured concerning the policy condition regarding holding of a valid licence by the driver or his qualification to drive during the relevant period, the insurer would not be allowed to avoid its liability towards the insured unless the said breach or breaches ... is/are so fundamental as are found to have contributed to the cause of the accident." "The insurer who is made statutorily liable to pay compensation to third parties on account of the certificate of insurance issued shall be entitled to recover any such sum from the insured if the insurer were not otherwise liable to pay such sum to the insured by virtue of the conditions of the contract of insurance indicated by the policy." Applying these principles to the facts, the Court found the driver lacked authorisation to drive a transport vehicle (breach of policy) but, in the interest of the victim and applying the pay-and-recover doctrine, directed the insurer to satisfy the award and thereafter recover the amount from the owner. The Tribunal's quantum award of Rs.19,59,895/- is confirmed. The appeal is partly allowed to the extent that the Appellant-Insurance Company is directed to pay the compensation with interest at 9% p.a. to the claimant and thereafter recover the amount from respondents 3 and 4 (owner/driver); the insurer must deposit the balance due within eight weeks. This is a Madras High Court appeal by United India Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.19,59,895 to V. Vijayakumar for catastrophic head injuries from a Maxi Cab accident on 07.07.2002. The insurer argued the driver only held an LMV licence and lacked the badge/authorisation to drive a transport vehicle, breaching policy conditions. The Court held the insurer had proved breach but applied the 'pay and recover' principle under Section 149: confirmed the compensation amount and directed the insurer to pay with 9% interest and recover from the owner. The claimant thus retains the award while the insurer may seek reimbursement from the insured. United India Insurance Company Ltd. v. V. Vijayakumar, High Court of Madras, C.M.A. No.3044 of 2005, judgment dated 08.09.2010. Accident on 07.07.2002 at Annur Bus Stand: Tourist Maxi Cab (Mahendra van Regn.No. TN 27 L 3638) hit pedestrian claimant causing severe head injury, diffuse axonal injury and 95% permanent disability. Claimant sought Rs.25,00,000; Tribunal awarded Rs.19,59,895 (breakdown: permanent disability Rs.8,20,800; medical Rs.9,37,095; loss of income Rs.1,00,000; pain/suffering and other heads). Key legal issues: validity of driver's licence/authorisation (LMV vs transport/badge), breach of policy condition under Section 149(2) M.V. Act 1988, burden on insurer to prove fundamental breach, and application of 'pay and recover' doctrine under Section 149 and Swaran Singh (2004) 3 SCC 297. Court relied on Swaran Singh, New India Assurance v. Kamla, Oriental Insurance v. Angad Kol, Kusum Rai, Laxmi Narain Dhut, Bhuvan Singh and others, held insurer proved lack of authorisation but nevertheless must pay award and may recover from owner; confirmed final compensation Rs.19,59,895 with interest at 9% p.a. V.Vijayakumar (1st Respondent / Claimant) Rs.19,59,895/- Rs.19,59,895/- 9% p.a. Tribunal heads maintained; Court reallocated 'mental agony' (Rs.75,000) into 'pain and suffering' and enhanced that head to Rs.1,00,000; total unchanged. Insurer directed to pay and recover from owner.
